claim: UNESCO reports Sub-Saharan Africa has highest gender gap in secondary education, 43% girls vs 50% boys completing lower secondary, rates below 20% in Sahel, and identifies school fees, early marriage, and safety as key barriers.

source.title: UNESCO Institute for Statistics — Education data and gender equity

method: Reviewed GEM Gender Report chapters on Sub-Saharan Africa, enrollment trends, and barrier analysis.
limitations: Completion data is modeled from surveys of variable recency.
Barrier classification is qualitative and may not capture local variation.

claim: The World Bank's Girls' Education program documents that school fee elimination, conditional cash transfers, and safety-oriented interventions have positive evidence for increasing girls' enrollment and retention, but implementation context determines effect sizes significantly.
method: Reviewed World Bank Girls' Education topic page for documented intervention evidence, enrollment trends, and barriers in Sub-Saharan Africa context.
limitations: World Bank topic page provides program-level evidence framing, not a formal systematic review with standardized effect sizes.

Prior evidence record (Baird et al. 2020, Taylor & Francis) URL was not programmatically accessible during verify:sources on 2026-06-07; the World Bank evidence page supports the same direction of evidence but at lower specificity.
Context-dependence of effect sizes is documented at program level, not through meta-analysis.
